title: Radiowave-induced Resistance Oscillations
authors: E. Bell, C. Hnatovsky, K. W. Baldwin, L. N. Pfeiffer, K. W. West, S. Studenikin, M. A. Zudov
abstract: Microwave-induced resistance oscillations (MIROs) \cite{zudov:2001a} occur when a 2D electron gas is subjected to radiation of frequency $ω= 2 πf$ and varying magnetic field $B$. MIROs are periodic in $1/B$, with the period determined by the radiation frequency $ω$, and their amplitude scales with the radiation power. Stepping from single-photon transitions between Landau levels, MIROs are found on the lower-field side of the cyclotron resonance, $ω_c \lesssim ω$, where $ω_c$ is the cyclotron frequency. Here, we report on experimental observation of another class of magneto resistance oscillations, which are also induced by radiation, but in the radio frequency (UHF band) range. These oscillations are distinct from MIROs in the following aspects: (i) they occur at $ω_c \gg ω$, (ii) their amplitude is independent of radiation power, (iii) their period is controlled by the radiation electric field, rather than by $ω$, and (iv) they can be either $1/B$ or $1/B^2$-periodic, depending on $B$. We further show that these oscillations can be explained by a displacement model in the limit of short-range disorder.
summary: This paper reports on Radiowave-induced Resistance Oscillations (RIROs) observed in a 2DEG, distinguishing them from standard MIROs by noting their occurrence when the cyclotron frequency is much larger than the radiation frequency. The authors demonstrate that these oscillations are governed by the electric field and can be explained theoretically using a displacement model incorporating short-range disorder effects.
main_problem: The paper investigates a new class of magneto-resistance oscillations, termed Radiowave-induced Resistance Oscillations (RIROs), which are distinct from the well-known Microwave-induced Resistance Oscillations (MIROs). It aims to characterize these oscillations under radiofrequency irradiation.
main_result: The authors report observing RIROs in a regime ($\omega_c \gg \omega$) where they are independent of radiation power and exhibit periodicities ($1/B$ or $1/B^2$) controlled by the electric field rather than the microwave frequency. The findings confirm a theoretical displacement model explanation.
method: The study involves measuring longitudinal resistivity ($ho$) as a function of magnetic field ($B$) while irradiating a 2DEG with radiofrequency radiation, and analyzing the dependence of oscillation periodicity on source power and frequency.
model_or_system: The physical system is a two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG) in a GaAs/AlGaAs quantum well structure. The theoretical explanation relies on a displacement model applied to photoresistance, particularly considering the contribution from short-range disorder.
key_observables: Longitudinal resistivity ($ho$) vs $B$, oscillation maxima ($\kappa$), extracted radiation electric field ($E$), and the number of participating photons ($N_{ph}$).
important_parameters: The ratio $\omega_c	au_{cr}$ (cyclotron frequency times scattering time), the radiation frequency ($\omega$), and the magnetic field dependence leading to crossovers between $B^{-2}$ and $B^{-1}$ periodicity.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the oscillations can be explained by a displacement contribution in the limit of short-range disorder, and it models the electric field extraction based on source power ($E \propto P_s^{1/2}$).
figures_summary: Figures show $ho$ vs $B$ demonstrating RIROs and zero-resistance states; plots illustrate the crossover of oscillation periodicity ($\kappa$) between $B^{-2}$ and $B^{-1}$ regimes at a critical point; and other figures confirm the scaling of the extracted electric field ($E \propto \sqrt{P_s}$) and photon number ($N_{ph}$).
paper_structure: The paper establishes the novelty of RIROs compared to MIROs by detailing their distinct physical conditions ($\omega_c \gg \omega$). It then presents experimental evidence showing power-independent amplitude, field-controlled periodicity, and a crossover in periodicity dependence on $B$. Finally, it connects these observations to a theoretical displacement model.
why_interesting: The work provides an excellent example of how nonlinear transport phenomena can be probed using external fields (RF radiation) in condensed matter systems. The clear distinction between MIROs and RIROs highlights the sensitivity of quantum transport measurements to both the frequency regime ($\omega$ vs $\omega_c$) and the underlying physical mechanism (single-photon vs displacement/disorder effects).
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Longitudinal resistivity ρ versus magnetic ﬁeld B measured under radiowaves of frequency f = 0.35 GHz at different source powers from Ps = −22 dBm (left curve) to Ps = −6 dBm (right curve), in a step of 4 dBm. The maxima of observed oscillations are marked by κ = 1, 2, 3, ... next to ρ(B) measured at Ps = −6 dBm. ZRS stands for zero-resistance state, developed at B > B1, where B1 is the ﬁeld of a fundamental RIRO maximum (κ = 1).

FIG. 2. Order of the oscillation maxima κ (solid circles) as a function of inverse magnetic ﬁeld 1/B obtained from the extrema of magnetoresistivity measured under radiowaves of frequency (a) f = 0.35 GHz and (b) f = 1.00 GHz at different source powers from Ps = −6 dBm (left) to Ps = −22 dBm (right), as marked. Solid lines are ﬁts to Eq. (2). Vertical line, drawn at ωcτcr = 1, il- lustrates a crossover between the κ ∝B−2, Eq. (3), and κ ∝B−1, Eq. (4), regimes.

FIG. 3. κ (solid circles) as a function of inverse magnetic ﬁeld 1/B obtained from the extrema of magnetoresistivity measured under ra- diowaves of frequency f = 0.35 GHz at Ps = −6 dBm (left) and Ps = −22 dBm (right), as marked. Dotted lines and dashed line represent ﬁts to Eq. (3) and Eq. (4), respectively. Vertical line, drawn at ωcτcr = 1, illustrates a crossover between the κ ∝B−2 (Eq. (3)) and κ ∝B−1 (Eq. (4)) regimes.

FIG. 5. Number of photons Nph = κωc/ω (solid circles) as a function of inverse magnetic ﬁeld 1/B obtained from the extrema of magnetoresistivity measured under radiowaves of frequency f = 0.35 GHz at Ps = −6 dBm and Ps = −22 dBm, as marked. Dotted line illustrates Nph ∝B−1 dependence expected for high power data at ωcτcr > 1, whereas the lower power data crosses over to expected saturation expected at ωcτcr < 1 (the dashed line is a guide to an eye), see Eq. (6). Vertical line is drawn at ωcτcr = 1. The cyclotron resonance takes place at 1/B ≈1.3 · 103 T−1.

FIG. 4. Radiowave electric ﬁeld E, obtained from Eq. (5), as a function of the source power Ps for f = 0.35 GHz (circles) and f = 1.00 GHz (squares). Solid line illustrates the expected E ∝P 1/2 s dependence. Inset shows ρ versus B measured at f = 9.5 GHz to obtain the effective mass m⋆used to calculate E.
